entry.title: How to Build SAP BTP Extensions with CAP — A Practical Developer’s Guide
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Develop extensions with CAP following the SAP BTP Developer's Guide course.

Learning outcome
After completing this course, you will be able to:

Create a Node.js-based extension from scratch with the  (CAP). You will enrich your extension with an SAP Fiori elements user interface, custom code, external service and security features.  Finally, deploying your extension and integrating your app into SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition. This course is also available in a self-paced e-learning format as part of a free SAP Learning Journey on learning.sap.com

 

Goals
  • Get started with SAP: Building the basics
  • Develop your expertise: Skill deepening learning
  • Excel in your expertise: Advanced specialization learning
 
Please post you question related to the digital course in the Q&A area. 

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! 
We are here to support you.


I appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards, Lian

entry.title: cds-toon-ai-protocol-adapter: a pragmatic way to cut AI token costs using SAP CAP
entry.content:
If you’ve been experimenting with AI in enterprise apps, you’ve probably felt the "token tax". It’s the silent cost hidden in every JSON payload you stream to an LLM. The more verbose the structure, the more you pay, both in latency and in cost.

This is exactly where the cds-toon-ai-protocol-adapter lands: a small, composable CAP plugin that brings the TOON (Token-Oriented Object Notation) protocol to your services. So you can keep your code, keep your patterns, and still send leaner payloads to AI systems.

In short:

  • It adds TOON as a protocol to CAP (REST and OData variants)
  • It automatically converts JSON ↔ TOON on the wire
  • It can auto-pick the most token-efficient response format
  • It logs token statistics so you can quantify the win

It's is directly inspired by an article I read on medium: "TOON, MCP and Agents: Less Hype, More Real Architecture (and a 25% Saving That Actually Matters)".

Toon procotol adapter in action

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Why Toon in CAP?

Let’s start with the pain:

  • JSON is great for machines and tools, but it’s verbose for LLMs
  • LLM pricing and latency scale with tokens, not bytes
  • Sending full JSON schemas, deeply nested objects, and descriptive keys is comfortable for us, but expensive for models

TOON (Token-Oriented Object Notation) flips that mindset:

  • It’s a compact, human-readable, model-friendly representation
  • It removes structural noise while keeping semantic meaning
  • It often reduces token counts by 20–50% compared to JSON

What the adapter gives you in CAP:

  • A protocol-level optimization, no refactors in service logic
  • Opt-in per service via @protocol annotations
  • Automatic conversion on responses (JSON → TOON), optional on requests (TOON → JSON)
  • A safety net: it can auto-decide per response if JSON is actually cheaper and choose that instead

So the real "why" is simple: you can introduce AI cost and latency optimizations where they belong, at the boundary, without touching domain logic, entities, or your CAP service handlers.

Where it fits in your architecture

If your CAP service:

  • Feeds agents with app context
  • Exposes APIs to AI tools or MCP-style execution flows

…then the adapter sits right at the edge of your service. Clients can consume Toon endpoints or keep using JSON/OData, your service handlers continue to speak plain JavaScript objects. Meanwhile, the adapter keeps score and trims the fluff.

How to use it (in less than 5 minutes)

1) Install

npm install @geertjanklaps/cds-toon-ai-protocol-adapter

Make sure you’re on @sap/cds >= 9.

2) Annotate your service with the Toon protocol

REST-style TOON:

@protocol: ['toon']
service MyToonService {
}

Prefer OData compatibility but want TOON on the wire?

@protocol: ['toon-odata']
service MyToonService {
}

3) Implement your service as usual

NoTOON-specific code needed. No new SDK. Your handlers stay clean.

4) Configure behavior in package.json

{
  "cds": {
    "toonAdapter": {
      "requestProcessingEnabled": false,
      "responseProcessingEnabled": true,
      "logTokenStatistics": true,
      "autoDetermineResponseContentType": true
    }
  }
}

What this means:

  • responseProcessingEnabled: true → your JSON responses can be converted to TOON
  • requestProcessingEnabled: false → clients can still send JSON; turn this on if you want to accept TOON requests too
  • autoDetermineResponseContentType: true → the adapter will send TOON or JSON based on which uses fewer tokens for that response
  • logTokenStatistics: true → you’ll see logs like: [toon] - Token count - JSON: 150, TOON: 95, Difference: 55 (36,7%)

5) Call the endpoints

  • REST base: /toon/
    • Example: GET /toon/my-toon-service/getData
  • OData base: /toon-odata/
    • Example: GET /toon-odata/my-toon-odata-service/getData

By default, clients can still send JSON. If you enable TOON requests, they can POST with Content-Type: text/toon.

When to use it

Ideal scenarios:

  • AI/LLM integrations where every token counts
  • Near-real-time experiences where shaving tokens reduces latency
  • Cost-sensitive workloads or multi-tenant setups where savings compound

Observed benefits:

  • Token reduction: typically 20–50% vs JSON
  • Lower bandwidth usage
  • Reduced AI API cost and faster roundtrips

This is not about "changing your data model for AI", it’s about optimizing the transport layer so your CAP services can collaborate with AI systems efficiently.

Practical tips:

  • Don’t refactor your code. Add the protocol, turn on responseProcessingEnabled, and measure
  • Keep autoDetermineResponseContentType enabled in production. Sometimes JSON is cheaper, let the adapter pick the winner
  • Enable logTokenStatistics in development and load tests. Record baseline token counts before/after
  • If you expose an MCP server using @gavdi/cap-mcp, use the toon-odata protocol

A minimal test you can run today

  1. Enable TOON on a read-heavy service
  2. Turn on logTokenStatistics
  3. Hit endpoints with realistic data (lists, expansions, nested structures)
  4. Compare JSON vs TOON token counts in logs
  5. Forward the same responses to your AI layer and measure end-to-end latency and cost

Expect measurable token drops on non-trivial payloads. In multi-tenant scenarios, those savings add up, quietly and meaningfully.

Closing thoughts

We don’t need more hype around "AI-native" architectures. We need small, composable decisions that make our systems cheaper, faster, and more maintainable. The cds-toon-ai-protocol-adapter does exactly that for CAP:

  • No rewrites.
  • Real savings.
  • Better UX for agents and LLMs.

If you’re serious about bringing AI into your SAP landscape, start where it hurts the budget: tokens on the wire. Add TOON at the protocol layer, measure, and move on to the next pragmatic win.

Useful links:

  • NPM: @geertjanklaps/cds-toon-ai-protocol-adapter
  • Issues and support: GitHub repository
  • Toon documentation: @toon-format/toon

If you try this in a multi-tenant setup or with MCP-heavy flows, I’d love to hear your numbers!

entry.title: SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) | Customer Roundtable | February 2026
entry.content:
CAP Customer Roundtable

Get ready for our first CAP Customer Roundtable of the year, coming this February! This event is an excellent opportunity to connect, explore what's new, and look ahead at what's coming for the CAP community.

This is your chance to connect with the CAP team and community to discuss recent developments and future directions. The session will include:

  • The latest CAP announcements
  • Product demos and presentations
  • An open forum for your topics and questions

To make this event as relevant as possible, we need your input. If you have a specific insight, challenge, or topic you want to see on the agenda, please reach out and let us know.

 

 

 Agenda

  1. Introduction
  2. Updates from CAP Team
    • Capire renovations and AI updates
    • Recent Node.js updates
    • Security updates
    • CAP content from the Developer Advocates
  3. Q&A / Topics by the Community

 

Meeting Information

When: Wednesday, February 18th, 2026, 11 AM EST | 5 PM CET

Where: Teams Meeting

Meeting Request Download: link

Who:  

    • All interested partners and stakeholders are invited to join: developers, enterprise architects, technical consultants…
    • The SAP Cloud Application Programming Model team
    • Anyone interested in learning about the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model and exchange your ideas and feedback with other users and the product team

Looking forward to seeing you there!

Also check out the Events page in our documentation and our Community page for updates on upcoming events.

entry.title: SAP CodeJam Roadshow 2026 - Brazil edition 🇧🇷 (Kicking off in March and continuing in April)
entry.content:
Oi SAP Developers!

I've got some exciting news to share with you. The SAP CodeJam roadshow is coming back to Latin America! This time,  we will do a special edition in Brazil.  We will have many stops on the roadshow starting the 1st week of March, doing CodeJams as part of SAP Inside Tracks, organised by local community members, and then continuing  in April. entry.title: Kyma Evolution: Transforming SAP Kyma into a tailor-made SaaS Platform for sbs extensions
entry.content:
Introduction

Hello SAP Community!

As an Extensibility Expert, I’m constantly looking for the most efficient ways to build and operate enterprise-grade extensions for the SAP Cloud ERP following the Clean Core principles. We all know the Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) is the go-to framework for Cloud Native side-by-side Extensions on BTP, but where you run them on large scale matters just as much as how you code it. 

Today, I’m starting a 3-part series (Part 2, Part 3 ) "Kyma Evolution". We will explore how the powercouple of Kyma in combination with the CAP Operator provides a scalable, high-performance, cost-effective runtime for CAP Multitenancy SaaS applications. In case you don’t know SAP Kyma and its benefits yet, please have a look at the Kyma Learning which explains it quite well.  

The paradigm shift: From monolithic to a modular environment

For a long time, SAP Kyma was seen as a fixed bundle of tools. You got everything, whether you needed it or not. That has changed. With the Kyma Module Concept, the platform is now modular and extensible.  Even more exciting is the introduction of Community Modules. This means Kyma is no longer limited to what SAP provides out-of-the-box. The community can now contribute extensions. One of the most powerful examples so far is the CAP Operator.  By adding the CAP Operator as a module, you transform a generic Kyma cluster into a specialized CAP SaaS Runtime.

The "Smart Broker": Why the CAP Operator is a game-changer

When we compare Kyma to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry, the advantages of using a dedicated Operator become clear. While Cloud Foundry is a great general-purpose platform, the CAP Operator on Kyma acts as a "smart broker" specifically for CAP Applications.

Key Benefits for Partners and Customers:

  • 100% Automated Lifecycle Management: The Operator understands CAP. It handles application deployment, tenant management, DB model updates, and service bindings automatically and new capabilities like SAP HANA Cloud Native tenants (Videocast) including tenant backup and restore management can be added once available.
  • Cost-Efficiency via Higher Container Density: In contrast to Cloud Foundry, Kyma allows for fine-grained resource limits. This means you can run more services on the same infrastructure, lowering your BTP bill when you are running multiple CAP Applications.
  • Cost-Efficiency via Resource Sharing: Expensive Services like SAP HANA Cloud and Cloud Logging can be shared in CF and Kyma. However we will see in Part 2 that in the Kyma environment this will be more elegant and simplified using the CAP Operator.
  • Enterprise Resilience & Day-2 Ops: It provides built-in "Reconciliation Loops." If a tenant's database connection fails, the Operator detects and fixes it without human intervention.
  • Advanced Networking & Security (Zero Trust Architecture): One of the most significant advantages over Cloud Foundry is how Kyma handles connectivity. In Cloud Foundry, preventing a service from being publicly accessible often requires manual effort. In Kyma, thanks to the CAP Operator's integration with Istio, your internal  services stay cluster-internal by default. You have full control over the attack surface.
  • Integrated Domain Management: Managing Custom Domains becomes a native experience using the CAP Operator. Instead of juggling external services for certificates, you can orchestrate your SaaS-brand URLs directly within the Kyma environment, providing a seamless, secure and automated experience.

The ecosystem enablers: Extensibility using CAP Plugins + Kyma Modules

SAP is providing enablers for the ecosystem on different levels:

  1. At the Infrastructure Level (Kyma): We use Modules (like the CAP Operator) to make the runtime "CAP-aware."
  2. At the Application Level (CAP): We use CAP Plugins to add business features like multitenancy or audit logging with a single command.
  3. At CAP CLI Level: We can add plugins like CAP Operator Plugin to the cds cli which provides capabilities to generate CAP Operator resources essential for deploying multi-tenant CAP Applications from your project setup.

This synergy creates the ultimate development and operations experience. You use plugins to build fast and the Operator to run smart and it enables you to contribute and to add your specific capabilities at any level.

Turning Kyma into a CAP Runtime

The beauty of this evolution is simplicity. You can now add the CAP Operator Community Module directly from your Kyma Cockpit using the Add Modules or Modify Modules Button on the Cluster Details Screen.

The Modules overview shows you the SAP Provided and Managed Modules under the Module Pane and the installed Community Modules in a separate Pane.

As a next step use the Add button on the Community Module Pane.

On the next screen you need to choose the Add Source YAML’s button to get the list of community modules. 

Just keep the defaults and press the Add button.

This will load the list of currently available Community Modules from which you need to check the cap-operator Tile and press Add. 

This will start the automatic installation of the CAP Operator Components into the namespace called cap-operator-system. 

The installation and module status will be displayed in the overview and will turn into green after installation is successfully finished.

By doing this, you aren't just deploying code; you are creating an intelligent system that manages your S/4HANA Multitenancy extensions built with CAP for you as it adds Custom Resources (CRs) like CAPApplication, CAPApplicationVersion, CAP Tenant and other to your cluster. This will enable: 

  • Quick and easy deployment of CAP application backends, router, and related networking components.
  • Integration with SAP Software-as-a-Service Provisioning service to handle asynchronous tenant subscription requests, executing provisioning / deprovisioning tasks as Kubernetes jobs.
  • Automated upgrades of known tenants as soon as new application versions are available.
  • Support for deployment of service-specific content / configuration as a Kubernetes job with every application version (for example, HTML5 application content to SAP HTML5 Application Repository Service).
  • Management of TLS certificates and DNS entries related to the deployed application, with support of customer-specific domains.

and further capabilities.

What’s Next?

In Part 2, we will dive deeper. We’ll look in more details of the runtime and at a cost benefits to prove why this modular approach is a win for your bottom line. What are your thoughts on the modular Kyma concept? Have you tried the CAP Operator yet? Have you ideas for additional community modules for the ecosystem?

entry.title: Part 2: Runtime Architecture & Cost efficiency gains
entry.content:
Introduction

In Part 1, we introduced the CAP Operator as the brain to make out of your Kyma Cluster a runtime for CAP Multitenancy SaaS applications. Today, we dive into technical architecture. We will see why Kyma isn't just "another place to run containers," but can be turned into a highly optimized environment that saves costs by being smarter about how it uses resources, especially when compared to a traditional Cloud Foundry setup, important if you need to run CAP Multitenancy Applications on a large scale.

1. The Core Architecture paradigm: Declarative Tenant Management

While Cloud Foundry relies on imperative commands (like cf push or cf deploy), the CAP Operator uses Custom Resources (CRs) the Kubernetes extensibility API for customization. The most important one for SaaS Multitenancy CAP Solutions is the CAPTenant.

In Cloud Foundry, the Service Manager handles the creation of HDI containers, and it does the same in Kyma. However, the CAP Operator adds a layer of "Operational Intelligence":

  • Autonomous Monitoring: The Operator doesn't just trigger the Service Manager; it monitors the entire lifecycle of the tenant's database schema. If a cds deploy (migration) fails, the Operator detects the "Unhealthy" state and can automatically retry or alert, integrated directly into the Kubernetes event stream.
  • State Reconciliation: It ensures that the state of your tenants always matches your configuration. If a tenant record is stuck, the Operator's reconciliation loop acts as an automated "Day-2" administrator.
  • Automatic creation of the Provider tenant: The CAP Operator will automatically take care that the provider tenant for your SaaS Application is created and updated like for the Subscriber tenants, whereas in Cloud Foundry you will need to to care. 

2. The "Zero-Idle" secret: On-Demand MTXS Pods

In a typical CAP multitenancy setup, the CAP MTXS component is the heavy lifter. It handles onboarding, unsubscription and database upgrades.

The Cloud Foundry Challenge: In CF, the MTXS logic often runs as one container or a permanent process within your application instance. This means you are constantly paying for the RAM and CPU of this "waiting" process, even if no one is subscribing to your app for days.

The Kyma + CAP Operator Advantage: The Operator implements what we call a "Zero-Idle" policy for provisioning tasks:

  • Trigger-based Lifecycle: When a subscription event occurs, the CAP Operator spins up a short-lived Job Pod specifically for the MTXS task of that tenant. For more details on how the CAP Operator manages the tenant lifecycle please have a look at the Tenant Provisioning Documentation.
  • Scale-to-Zero: With the CAP Operator you can run upgrades of your database tenants in parallel. Once the tenant is successfully managed and the database tenant is ready, the MTXS Pod is terminated.
  • Cost Benefit: You pay zero for the MTXS infrastructure during idle times. In large-scale landscapes with many extensions, this optimization can reduce your runtime memory footprint by 20–30%.

3. Scaling without linear cost growth

A common concern is that Kubernetes might be more expensive than Cloud Foundry. The reality is the opposite when scaling SaaS:

  1. Bin-Packing: Kubernetes allows for "Overcommitting." Since your applications and tenants aren't all active at the same millisecond, you can pack more application instances onto a single K8S Node than the rigid memory cells of CF allow.
  2. Resource Pooling: By using the CAP Operator to orchestrate the Service Manager, you can efficiently fill up large, cost-effective HANA Cloud instances. You avoid the "base cost" of having many small, underutilized HANA instances.
  3. Efficiency Gains: As you grow from 10 to 100 tenants, your infrastructure costs grow at a much flatter angle because the "management overhead" (like MTXS) only consumes resources when it's actually working.

4. Centralized Observability: Selective Logging

Logging is often an overlooked cost driver. While both BTP environments use the SAP Cloud Logging Service which can be shared among multiple deployments, the way how it's done and how they handle logging data (storage as the major cost driver) is fundamentally different.

  • Cloud Foundry: Every app instance pushes all logs blindly to the service. You have little control over the volume.
  • Kyma (The Daemon Approach): Kyma uses the Telemetry Module (Fluent Bit) running as a daemon on every node. 
    • Pre-Filtering: You can define a LogPipeline to filter logs before they leave the cluster. For example, you can exclude send logs from specific production namespaces or filter the logs by specific attributes.
    • Centralized Sharing: You don't need to bind every app. You define one central pipeline that securely routes logs from the entire cluster to a single Cloud Logging instance, providing a unified view with significantly lower ingest costs.

On how to use the SAP Cloud Logging with SAP Kyma, please have a look into the Kyma Integration with SAP Cloud Logging Blog.

Summary: The Architect's choice

Choosing Kyma and the CAP Operator isn't just about a "new technology." It's a strategic decision to move from reserved resources to on-demand resources and how to utilize them more efficiently.

  • CF: Predictable, but you often pay for "idling" capacity.
  • Kyma + CAP Operator: Dynamic, ensuring you only pay for what is actively serving your business or your tenants.

What’s Next?

Now that we have seen how the Power couple ”BTP Kyma&CAP Operator” enables you to lower your infrastructure cost in case of running CAP applications on large scale, we will see in Part 3 how the CAP Operator helps you to run your Application Lifecycle Management 100% automated which is a further important pillar to reduce your overall TCO.

entry.title: Github Copilot VSCode - My Own SAP Development Rules
entry.content:
Lightweight Governance for SAP CAP and UI5 Projects

Creating clear, consistent project rules is one of the simplest ways to improve collaboration and quality in SAP CAP and UI5 projects. In this workspace, the guidance is organized into focused files: backend rules for CAP, frontend rules for UI5, Jest testing rules, and Bruno collection rules. Each file sets a crisp scope and avoids ambiguity, which is essential when multiple developers or tools contribute changes.

Why this structure works

- **CAP rules** enforce reusable types, managed data, soft-delete behavior, and strict service conventions.
- **UI5 rules** define naming standards, view constraints, and annotation boundaries, keeping frontend development aligned with backend services.
- **Testing rules** establish baseline quality by requiring meaningful Jest coverage for custom logic.
- **Bruno rules** standardize API collection formats, which keeps integration testing reliable and portable.

Together, these files form a lightweight governance layer. They improve onboarding, reduce review cycles, and keep the codebase predictable. For teams working across SAP CAP and SAP UI5, this approach offers a practical template for consistency without sacrificing speed.

Call to action

If your team ships SAP CAP and UI5 solutions, consider creating a similar ruleset and share your approach with the SAP Community. Small, well-scoped guidelines can make a big difference in day-to-day delivery.

Download, Use and Improve the rules:
https://github.com/radu103/github-copilot-vscode-sap-dev-instructions/

entry.title: Declarative Programming in CAP: Less Code, More Value
entry.content:
The Declarative Approach

Ever found yourself writing repetitive validation handlers or debugging complex imperative code? Declarative programming in SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) offers a better way. Declarative programming means describing what you want to achieve rather than how to achieve it. Instead of writing step-by-step instructions, you express the problem itself and let the system determine the solution automatically.

There are a couple of benefits using a declarative approach:

  • Improved Readability: Code that focuses on intent rather than implementing step-by-step instructions is clearer and easier to understand
  • Enhanced Maintainability: When rules live close to the data they govern, logic is easier to find and update
  • Reduced Boilerplate: Minimize repetitive code, streamline development, and reduce bug potential

In CAP, developers can declaratively define numerous CAP features - including queries and view definitions, input validations or annotation based constraints. The result? Less imperative code, consistent validations between backend and OData annotations, and logic that is intuitive to understand. This post walks you through the core concepts of declarative programming in CAP, covering declarative expressions, different kinds of input validations, how to use expressions as annotation values and the new @assert feature. These techniques work in both CAP Java and CAP Node.js.

 

Declarative Programming in CAP: Core Concepts

Declarative Expressions

With CAP, you can use declarative expressions in queries, view definitions, and calculated elements by defining them directly in your model. For example, you can use the exists predicate to select only those authors whose associated books contain elements with a price greater than 20:

Select from Authors { name } where exists books[price > 20.00]

In another example, you can use declarative expressions to compute free seats on a flight:

entity Flights : cuid {
  ...
  max_seats : Integer;
  occupied_seats : Integer;
  free_seats : Integer = max_seats - occupied_seats;
}

In all cases you can reduce the need for imperative code by using declarative expressions.

Please read our documentation for further information about views and projections and calculated elements.

Input Validations

Instead of writing custom event handlers for every validation, CAP provides several built-in annotations that handle common scenarios declaratively. These validations are executed at the database layer, reducing round trips and enabling efficient error aggregation:

  • @assert.format – validates that data matches regular expressions
  • @assert.range – validates that data is within a given range
  • @assert.target – validates that the target of an association exists
  • @mandatory – validates that a value is provided
  • @​readonly – ensures that a value is not modified
  • The .message suffix allows custom error messages instead of generic text

In the following example, you check that a description has a minimum length of 3 characters:

Description @assert.format: '.{3,}'  @assert.format.message: 'Description too short’

In CAP, Core Data Services (CDS) is used to define service definitions, data models, queries, and expressions. Using declarative annotations in CDS models reduces custom logic while keeping validations consistent across services and UI.

Learn more about event handlers in CAP Java and events in CAP Node.js  , read our documentation on declarative constraints and check out DJ Adams’ blog post Constraints, expressions and axioms in action.

Expressions as Annotation Values

Annotations can use CXL (CDS Expression Language) expressions as values. This is particularly useful for instance-based authorizations. The following code snippet ensures that users can only read, update, or delete orders they created:

annotate Orders with @(restrict: [
  { grant: ['READ', 'UPDATE', 'DELETE'], where: (createdBy = $user) }
]);

Using parentheses ensures that the CDS compiler can validate and optimize the expression.

You can also use dynamic expressions in UI annotations. The compiler translates these expressions automatically to the corresponding OData annotations:

 @​UI.Hidden: (status <> 'visible')

Learn more about expressions as annotation values.

If you are interested to learn more about CXL check out this mini-series on CXL by our Developer Advocate DJ Adams and Patrice Bender.

The New @assert Annotation

The @assert annotation is a new feature in CAP, released in December 2025. It enables complex, custom validations using CXL. This annotation provides a powerful way to enforce specific checks on inputs and raise appropriate errors if those checks fail. Error messages can be configured as either static strings or i18n message keys.

The annotation takes the form @assert: (<constraints>), where <constraints> are standard SQL case expressions containing one or more 'when' branches. The following example shows how to validate that a description is specified correctly:

annotate TravelService.Travels with {
  Description @assert: (case
    when Description is null then 'Description must be specified'
    when trim(Description) = '' then 'Description must not be empty'
    when length(Description) < 3 then 'Description too short'
  end);                                                              
}

It is also possible to formulate complex validations that would typically require a lot of code. The following example validates that the end date of a trip is not before the begin date and that all flight bookings fall within the travel period:

EndDate @assert: (case
 when EndDate < BeginDate then 'End Date must be after Begin Date'
 when exists Bookings [Flight.date > Travel.EndDate] then 'All bookings must be within the travel period'
end);

The new @assert feature enables you to leverage the full potential of the CXL expression language while ensuring that validations are executed efficiently directly on the database. It integrates tightly with the Fiori user interface, providing rich error feedback and reducing the need for custom code.  

 

Conclusion

Embracing declarative programming in CAP can transform the way you develop cloud applications. By reducing the need for custom code, ensuring consistency, and facilitating the expression of business logic, you can efficiently create applications that are easy to maintain and intuitive. Whether you're new to CAP or a seasoned developer, consider exploring the benefits of declarative programming. Start by reading our documentation.

Happy coding!

entry.title: SAP Developer News, February 5th, 2026
entry.content:
DESCRIPTION

SAP Build Q4 2025 Release Highlights

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/tooling-sap-build-blog-posts/what-s-new-in-sap-build-q4-2025-release-highlights/bc-p/14316927

My Home in SAP BTP ABAP Environment

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/my-home-in-sap-btp-abap-environment/ba-p/14314918

 

Did You Know? #2 – Mocking Auth in CAP

Devtoberfest 2025 – Winners Announcement

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-blog-posts/devtoberfest-2025-winners-announcement/ba-p/14320779

New live stream series and blog post series on CAP topics

  • Modules, modularity & reuse in CDS models (blog post series) https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2026/01/01/modules-modularity-and-reuse-in-cds-models/
  • Hands-on SAP Dev CXL live stream series playlist https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQCEU8XcyqnA5wYEZGxMPm6B
  • A new Hands-on SAP Dev mini-series on the core expression language in CDS (blog post) https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2025/12/09/a-new-hands-on-sap-dev-mini-series-on-the-core-expression-language-in-cds/
  • Constraints, expressions and axioms in action (blog post) https://qmacro.org/blog/posts/2026/01/27/constraints-expressions-and-axioms-in-action/

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 SAP Build Q4 2025 Release Highlights

1:36 My Home in SAP BTP ABAP Environment

3:04 DYK #2 Mocking Auth in CAP

4:10 Devtoberfest 2025 – Winners Announcement

4:44 New live stream series and blog post series on CAP topics

Transcription

00;00;00;29 - 00;00;05;28
Announcer Guy
This is the SAP Developer News for February 5th, 2026.

00;00;06;00 - 00;00;09;25
Shrini Neelamegam
Q4 of 2025 was a huge season for SAP Build,

00;00;09;27 - 00;00;40;11
Shrini Neelamegam
and we have got some powerful new features to talk about. First up, the agent builder in Joule Studio is officially GA. With MCP server integration and the ability to link multiple agents together. You can now build production grade AI agents that actually moves the needle for your business. Plus, Joule is now baked into every edition of Work Zone, bringing context aware answers and proactive insights right to your team's fingertips.

00;00;40;13 - 00;01;07;17
Shrini Neelamegam
For the developers out there, you can now spin up Freestyle UI5 apps in SAP Build Code with a simple slash command. And if you are looking to automate, you have added new ways to trigger processes and build process automation, making it easier to bridge the gap between SAP and non SAP systems. On the design front end SAP mobile start just got a makeover with Apple's liquid glass look.

00;01;07;20 - 00;01;35;14
Shrini Neelamegam
And for the admins, Workzone sites can now handle thousands of documents and translations. We are just scratching the surface here. There is plenty more on decision tables, decision rules, HTML templates for notifications, and new CAPannotations. Head over to Frederick's latest blog post for full Q4 2025 release announcement.

00;01;35;17 - 00;02;07;13
Sheena M K
SAP has rolled out My Home, the new personalized, light entry page for the SAP BTP ABAP Environment. By default, from at least 2602, and it will be the one and only entry page from release 2608. My home is designed to boost productivity with a modular, customizable layout that includes key sections such as news pages, add and insights, and cards, all aimed at giving users quick access to the tools and information they interact with the most.

00;02;07;15 - 00;02;40;00
Sheena M K
While the new section keeps you updated with the latest company or system updates, beta section offers quick access to your most used or favorite pages. The apps section improves discoverability with favorite, recent and most used apps. Plus, AI powered recommendations based on role assignments via SAP Business AI. The inside 30s provide immediate visibility into KPIs and analytics and data, offering quick access to apps for further details.

00;02;40;03 - 00;03;06;08
Sheena M K
Following the “Make It Mine” approach, users can hide, reorder and restructure sections. The skew then working side across all devices. So start exploring my home for a clean, organized and smarter navigation for the SAP BTP ABAP Environment. And for details, check out the blog post from Laura in the description below.

00;03;06;10 - 00;03;33;19
DJ Adams
Did you know that CAP has a mocked authentication strategy you can use in local development? So you don't have to put off this important part of your domain design? Here's a simple service annotated with some auth requirements. Authentication is required generally, and there are some role based restrictions on one of the entities. With CDS watch we get this strategy out of the box, as we can see, along with some users and roles too.

00;03;33;21 - 00;04;01;05
DJ Adams
Here's Alice with the admin role. For example. Let's explore. How about trying to read the Book Stocks Entity? 401! We need to authenticate, of course. Let's authenticate as Alice. Now we have read access. What about write? 403. Nope. Alice doesn't have the requisite role. Let's fix that with some extra config in dot cdsrc dot Json, for example, which assigns her the back office role that's required.

00;04;01;08 - 00;04;09;21
DJ Adams
Okay, let's try again. Success output request as Alice is now accepted. Nice.

00;04;09;23 - 00;04;31;17
Kevin Kiedelsheimer
Hey, SAP Community, it's finally time! We have the full list of the ten Devtoberfest 2025 winners ready and online. You can find a link to the blog post down in the description of this video, so you can head over to the SAP community and see if you are one of the approved winners of last year's Devtoberfest.

00;04;31;20 - 00;04;42;27
Kevin Kiedelsheimer
And if you're one of the winners, you can look forward to a great fully paid trip to SAP TechEd this year. I can't wait to see you there!

00;04;43;00 - 00;05;21;13
DJ Adams
The developer advocates mission is to help developers. That's you be successful. To that end, we create lots of content in various forms CodeJam, tutorials, videos, talks, and lots more. We just wanted to highlight a couple of recent series in the CAP area. First, there's a blog post series on the topic of CDS modeling. The series is called Modules Modularity and Reuse in CDS models, and it explores how we can use modules for reuse in and across our projects and beyond.

00;05;21;15 - 00;05;53;15
DJ Adams
It illustrates this by first creating and publishing a simple passive reuse module before moving on to understand, explore and dig into the active Capire common reuse module. We're also running a series right now on our hands on SAP Devs live stream show. It's on CAP’s Expression Language, C XL, and we're very happy to have Patrice Bender from the Cap compiler team take us through the beautiful detail in this show and tell style series.

00;05;53;17 - 00;06;21;10
DJ Adams
As always, driven by curiosity and the desire to understand. By the way, there's also a related blog post just published that explains some of the amazing detail that Patrice revealed in showing how constraints are realized by pushing down to the database. This week sees the third livestream episode in this series. You can join live at the usual hands on SAP Dev time.

00;06;21;10 - 00;06;29;09
DJ Adams
It's Friday mornings in Europe, and of course you can always watch the replays at your leisure links in the description.

entry.title: CAP Heaven? Not Yet — But You Can See It from Here
entry.content:
I've been building things on the server side for a long time. I started with Java, spent years as a solution architect on Azure, and served as product owner for an AWS-based product. Somewhere along the way, I also became deeply embedded in the SAP world — ABAP, Fiori, RAP, the whole ecosystem.

So when I first looked at CAP I wasn't seeing it with fresh eyes. I was seeing it through the lens of someone who'd already lived through the cloud-native revolution on other platforms. Someone who knew what good looked like, and who'd felt the pain of what didn't work.

And CAP impressed me. Not because it's perfect, but because it's making the right bets.

Why does this matter now?

More customers are moving to clean core strategies, and side-by-side extensibility is no longer optional — it's becoming the default approach. At the same time, SAP is finally aligning with mainstream cloud-native patterns in a way that feels genuine rather than cosmetic. If you're involved in SAP development today, CAP is where these threads converge.

An Opinionated Framework — With Good Opinions

CAP is opinionated. And I mean that as the highest compliment.

In software development, "opinionated" sometimes gets thrown around like an insult. But anyone who's spent time in the SAP ecosystem knows the cost of unopinionated — the wild west of custom code where every team invents their own patterns, their own naming conventions, their own architectural mistakes. I've inherited enough legacy code to know that freedom without guidance is just chaos with extra steps.

CAP has opinions about how you model your data (CDS), how you define your services, how you handle persistence, and how you deal with events. And they're good opinions. They're the kind of opinions that save you from yourself at 2 AM when you're tempted to take a shortcut that your future self will curse.

The CDS-first approach forces you to think about your domain model before you start coding behavior. The service layer gives you a clean separation between what you expose and how things work internally. The event-driven model nudges you toward loosely coupled designs. None of this is revolutionary — I've lived with these patterns in Spring Boot for years, and the Rails community figured this out ages ago — but for the SAP ecosystem, it feels like a breath of fresh air.

Coming from Java and Node.js, CAP's conventions feel instantly familiar. Convention over configuration. Sensible defaults. Guardrails that keep you on the happy path. The difference is that CAP brings this philosophy to a world that desperately needs it — a world where too many projects have paid the price of building without shared conventions.

A typical example: say you're building a side-by-side extension that enriches sales orders with external credit scoring data. In CAP, you define your domain model in CDS, project a service on top, register an event handler that calls the external scoring API on each read, and you're done. The framework handles the OData exposure, the error handling patterns, and the integration with SAP's event mesh. You focus on the business logic, not the plumbing. That's what good opinions buy you.

So Who Should Become a CAP Developer?

The Backend Developer

If you're coming from a traditional ABAP background, CAP will feel both familiar and foreign. The data modeling in CDS? You'll get that immediately — it's an evolution of what you already know. The service definitions? Similar enough to OData exposure patterns. But the runtime is Node.js or Java, and that's where the culture shock hits.

For me, the Node.js runtime was a natural choice. With a background in Java and JavaScript, picking up CAP's event handler patterns and the async-first model felt right at home. There's something satisfying about the lightweight, expressive nature of JavaScript for service development — and the Node.js ecosystem brings a speed of iteration that's hard to beat. If you're an ABAP developer without that background, though, be prepared: suddenly you're in a world of npm install, package.json, and asynchronous programming. The muscle memory you've built over years doesn't quite apply, and that's humbling — but also energizing once it clicks.

But here's what SAP developers bring to the table that's genuinely hard to replace: business understanding. You know what a sales order really is. You know why the pricing procedure matters. You know the fifty edge cases that will surface in production. That knowledge is pure gold in CAP development, and no amount of Node.js expertise can substitute for it.

The Frontend Developer

If you're a frontend developer building Fiori apps or React SPAs, CAP gives you a backend that's actually pleasant to work with — clean, predictable OData V4 services. But understanding why the business logic needs to work a certain way takes time, and that matters more than people think.

The Non-SAP Developer

CAP is built on Node.js and Java — technologies that millions of developers already know. Any competent full-stack developer could pick up CAP and start building. And they absolutely should.

The learning curve isn't the framework itself — it's the domain.

SAP isn't just a database with an API — it's decades of encoded business processes.

A strong Node.js developer will build something that runs, but understanding the process semantics, compliance requirements, and integration patterns takes patience and curiosity. The good news is that CAP's conventions make this ramp-up smoother than it's ever been, and developers who invest in understanding the SAP business context bring a valuable outside perspective.

The Real Answer

The best CAP developers, in my experience, are hybrids. People who understand the SAP business domain deeply and are willing to embrace modern development practices. That's a rare combination today, but it doesn't have to stay rare. It does require intentional investment — this isn't a simple reskilling exercise, it's a fundamental and exciting shift in how we think about SAP development. The good news? People who make this journey become incredibly valuable, because they can speak both languages fluently.

The Side-by-Side Struggle: When the APIs Aren't There

SAP's vision for extensibility is clear: keep the core clean, build extensions side-by-side on BTP, integrate through well-defined APIs. It's a sound architecture — and one that's getting more practical with every release. But it does come with challenges worth discussing openly.

The API Gap

The fundamental promise of side-by-side extension is that you can access the data and functionality you need through released APIs. And SAP has made real progress here — the SAP Business Accelerator Hub keeps growing, and many core business objects now have well-documented, stable APIs.

That said, you will encounter situations where the API you need doesn't exist yet, or doesn't cover the specific aspect of a business object your extension requires. When that happens, you need to get creative. In my experience, teams typically choose between three approaches:

  • Custom API exposure — build a thin wrapper on the S/4HANA side to expose exactly what you need. Fast to implement, but adds to the on-stack footprint you're trying to minimize.
  • Event-based replication — subscribe to business events and maintain a local projection of the data you need. More decoupled, but requires thinking carefully about data freshness and consistency.
  • Data products via SAP Business Data Cloud — when available for your domain, this is increasingly the cleanest path (more on this below).

None of these are one-size-fits-all, and the right choice depends on your latency requirements, data volume, and how close to clean core you need to stay. But they're solvable problems, and with every S/4HANA release, the API surface grows. The direction is clearly right — it's the pace that sometimes challenges project timelines.

The Data Volume Problem

Even when APIs exist, there's another challenge: data volume. Many real-world extensions need historical sales orders, inventory movements, customer interactions — potentially millions of records. APIs are designed for transactional access, not bulk data extraction. That's a reasonable design choice, but it means you need a different strategy.

SAP Business Data Cloud: A Promising Direction

This is where I want to mention something I've been exploring in tutorials but haven't yet used in a real project: SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) and its concept of data products.

The idea is compelling. Data products are curated, SAP-managed packages of business data — not just raw tables, but complete datasets with semantics, metadata, and relationships included. SAP handles extraction, harmonization, and delta replication. You activate a data product, and the data flows. For CAP developers, HANA Cloud can now expose these data products as virtual tables, opening a path to consuming vast amounts of business data without building custom replication pipelines.

BDC is still maturing — the data product catalog is growing, and the integration story with CAP continues to evolve. But if it delivers on its promise, it could fundamentally change the side-by-side equation. Instead of each project building its own data plumbing, SAP would provide a managed data layer that treats business data as a first-class product. That's worth keeping a close eye on.

A Realistic Perspective

I believe in the side-by-side model. Clean core is sound engineering, and it's getting more practical with every release. If you're starting a side-by-side project today, budget for integration complexity and be prepared to build a few bridges where the paved road hasn't been laid yet. But the direction is right, and tools like BDC suggest SAP is serious about closing the gaps.

So Is It Heaven?

Not yet. But you can see it from here.

CAP represents something genuinely important in the SAP world: a modern, principled approach to building extensions and applications. It respects developers' time with good conventions. It bridges the gap between SAP's business depth and modern development practices. And with initiatives like SAP Business Data Cloud on the horizon, the pieces are starting to come together.

The journey isn't easy. The competence challenge is real. The integration gaps require patience. But as someone who's built on Java, worked as an architect on Azure, shipped products on AWS, and spent years deep in SAP — I can tell you that CAP is the first thing in the SAP development world that feels like it genuinely belongs in the same conversation as modern cloud-native development.

It's not there yet. But it's heading in the right direction. And that makes me excited to write code again.

I'd love to hear your perspective: where do you see the biggest blocker today — skills, APIs, or data access? And if you've started your own CAP journey, what surprised you most? Let's discuss in the comments.

entry.title: What's new in SAP Cloud Application Programming Model – January 2026
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

We are excited to introduce our December 2025 and January 2026 releases of the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP). Our highlights include the new @assert feature, our revised CAP documentation, several enhancements to the CDS editor, and new features for deploying your CAP applications to the SAP BTP, Kyma Runtime.

Whether you are just getting started with CAP or are an experienced CAP developer, these updates aim to make your development experience smoother and more efficient. Stay tuned for more updates and mark your calendars for our upcoming events, including the first CAP Customer Roundtable of 2026 and Code Connect 2026.

 

Simplified Assertions with the New @assert Annotation

Declarative programming focuses on describing the intent rather than describing how to achieve it. This reduces the need for custom code, ensures consistency, and facilitates the expression of business logic. CAP offers numerous features supporting declarative programming. For a detailed overview read the following blog post: Declarative Programming in CAP: Less Code, More Value.

The December 2025 release of CAP introduces a new declarative feature that drastically reduces the amount of custom code needed in many applications. With the @assert annotation, you can now define more complex validation logic within your CDS definitions, eliminating the need for additional code. The @assert annotation supports the 'case when' syntax, allowing multiple validations to be executed sequentially. Furthermore, the returned error messages can be customized to be either a static string or a message key for internationalization (i18n) support. This enhancement helps to maintain data integrity and to provide clear and early feedback to users (for example by editing data in a draft) where validation rules are violated.

Read our documentation to learn all the details about the new @assert feature.

 Screenshot: The new @assert feature

 

Revised CAP Documentation: Finding Your Way Just Got Easier

In our latest release, we've refined and streamlined our CAP documentation to make it easier to find relevant information quickly.  We reorganized the docs into Get Started, Develop, and Deploy, so you can jump straight to the phase you’re in. Many guides were refreshed to match current CAP features and best practices, and new ones were added where we saw gaps.  Here are some key changes and additions you'll find:

Get Started Section

The initial guides received substantial updates. In a Nutshell now emphasizes staying current with CAP releases while streamlining setup instructions. The Bookshop Sample has been rewritten to align with current best practices. The Learn More section highlights most recommended learning resources, including Featured Samples and the qmacro series, making it clearer where to invest your learning time. Get Help includes an overview of main support channels and an improved troubleshooting guide.

Database Guides

Three new guides have been added addressing the following topics: CAP-Level Database Support, CDL Compiled to DDL, and Adding Initial Data.

Integration Section

This is an entirely new section. We've consolidated guidance on service integration, data federation, and inner-loop development. If you've been using the old Consuming (Remote) Services guide, you'll find its successor here with updated approaches.

Security & Data Privacy

This section has been restructured completely with new chapters on Authentication focusing on IAS, assigned user claims like AMS-Policies, and Outbound Authentication.

These updates aim to make your experience with our CAP documentation smoother and more efficient. We hope you find these enhancements useful, and as always, your feedback is invaluable to us.  

 

CDS Editor Updates

Our January 2026 release brings several enhancements to the CDS editor, designed to further enhance your development experience. Here’s what’s new:

  • Multi-Cursor Support for Selection Ranges: Selection ranges now also work with VS Code's multi-cursor mode, enabling you to edit multiple lines simultaneously.

 Screenshot: Multi-cursor support

  • Folding Ranges Improvements: Consecutive using statements are now treated as a single collapsible block, reducing clutter when folding code sections.

 Screenshot: Expanded using statement

 Screenshot: Collapsed using statement

  • Faster References Search and Workspace Symbols (Beta): Find References and Workspace Symbols operations now utilize a persisted index, which can significantly improve performance in larger projects with multiple independent CDS files.
    To enable this beta feature, set cds.workspace.persistency.enabled in the settings of your IDE. We're interested in your feedback on performance improvements or any issues you encounter.
  • Organized User Settings: Navigating user settings is now simpler with category grouping. The editor organizes settings into categories, making it easier to find and configure the settings you need.

 Screenshot: User settings organized in categories

 We hope these enhancements will make your development process smoother and more efficient. We welcome your feedback and suggestions as we continue to improve the CDS editor.

 

New features for Kyma Deployment

Our January release brings several new features for deploying your CAP applications to the SAP BTP, Kyma Runtime. For multitenant Kyma applications, we’ve introduced the new mtx-upgrade task, which is automatically added as a Kubernetes job by cds add multitenancy. This feature simplifies consistent upgrades across multiple tenants.

Another new addition is cds debug for Kubernetes. This command enables you to open a Chrome Node.js debugging session for your remote application running in Kyma. To see the available applications, use kubectl get deployments.

Example: run cds debug bookshop-srv --k8s to start a debugging session.

 Screenshot: New features for Kyma Deployment

These additions streamline both the deployment workflow for multitenant scenarios and the debugging process for applications running in Kyma.

 

Expert Session: Getting Started with CAP Node.js

Join us for an insightful session with expert Daniel Schlachter, in conversation with DJ Adams, as they guide you through embarking on your CAP Node.js project journey. This session covers essential topics such as initialization, data modelling, the cds command line tool, service creation, data mocking, annotations, event handlers, and declarative definitions. You will gain practical insights into best practices for CAP development that will help you structure your CAP projects efficiently from the start.

Don't miss this opportunity to learn from the experts and elevate your development practices.

 

Events

Next customer roundtable

The first CAP Customer Roundtable in 2026 is coming up soon! We invite you to join us on February 18th, 2026, at 5.00 PM CET. As always, we will provide you an overview of some of the latest features, insightful demos, and discussions on questions from the CAP community. Please refer to this blog post for additional details, including the agenda and dial-in information.

 

Mark your Calendars for Code Connect 2026

Code Connect returns for its third edition from July 13-16, 2026, in St. Leon-Rot, Germany. Online participation is also possible. The conference brings together UI5con, reCAP, and HANA Tech Con, offering a comprehensive program for SAP developers. Whether you are experienced or new to SAP technologies, Code Connect provides an opportunity to engage with peers, share knowledge, and explore the latest in SAP development.

The call for proposals is now open! We invite you to submit your own ideas and share your expertise with the community. Don’t wait and submit your session proposal by 13 March 2026 at the latest.

  • Call for proposals UI5con
  • Call for proposals reCAP
  • Call for proposals HANA Tech Con

Additionally, we are seeking sponsors to help make this event even better. Read our sponsorship prospectus to check our sponsorship opportunities. If you or your organization are interested in supporting Code Connect 2026, we'd love to hear from you.

Find all the details in this blog post and make sure to mark your calendars for this great event!

 

For further information on new features, please have a look at the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model Release Notes. If you want to stay up-to-date with news, learning resources, and product and strategy updates for CAP: follow our tag in the SAP Community.

We look forward to seeing how you use these new capabilities to create innovative solutions and drive your projects forward. Stay tuned for more updates and happy coding! 😊

entry.title: Ever wondered how side‑by‑side extensibility really works on SAP BTP?
entry.content:
Then our new Learning Journey Building Side‑by‑Side Extensions on SAP BTP on SAP Learning: is for you!
This journey is designed for developers, architects, and technical professionals who want to build clean, cloud‑ready extensions while embracing modern SAP development practices.

Discover how to create modern, scalable applications using CAP, SAP Business Application Studio, SAPUI5, and secure integrations with SAP S/4HANA and other SAP solutions.

⭐ What you’ll learn

  • Key concepts of side-by-side extensibility
  • Developing CAP-based services and UIs
  • Connecting securely to SAP systems
  • Deploying and operating apps on SAP BTP

Start your journey and take the next step in SAP BTP development! 
Earn your badge - Prove your skills and earn a certification that validates your expertise.

entry.title: The 2025 Developer Insights Survey: The Report
entry.content:
Role Overlaps for external SAP developers

Part I: Introduction and General Information​

Welcome to the 2025 SAP Developer Insights Survey report. This annual survey targets SAP’s existing external developer audience. It is used to track demographics, usage patterns, and trends within that population. 2025 is the fifth year we have conducted the survey. The survey program is led by the SAP Ecosystem and Community team. Key support is provided by the SAP Global Experience Management Office (XMO). Several other SAP teams provide advice and inputs to survey content. Those teams are listed at the end of this report.

The 2025 survey was open for six weeks - from January 22nd through March 7th.

Using social media announcements, SAP's Developer News show, and a web-popup on Community web sites, we invited all technical professionals in the SAP ecosystem to participate in the survey.

Each year's survey is composed of a mix of new topic questions and repeat select questions from past years to track trends.

Key Findings from the 2025 Survey​

  • Generative AI is Positively Affecting our Developer Ecosystem - While these tools are not exclusively Joule-based, roughly half of our respondents report evaluating Gen AI developer tooling and, among those using it, over half report that these tools have a "profound positive impact" in their real world use.

  • Low Code Use is Increasing - SAP Build low code tools lead in this space. Overall use of Low Code products on production projects continues to increase.

  • BTP Guidance Framework - limited awareness -- this important guidance document was announced at TechEd this past November, but - as with BTP Developers Guide in 2024 - barely half of respondents were aware of this document's existence.

  • Work-from-Home Continues Trending Down - we have been tracking these numbers since 2021. 6% fewer folks used their home as their primary workplace when compared to last year.

These topics will be covered in more detail in the rest of the report.

General Topics​

We included a number of general questions in the survey covering such topics as age, work location and status, as well as role.

Employment Status and Relation to SAP​

Employment

"What is your current employment status?"  
SAP Developer Community Composition
We have seen no significant change in these breakdowns from year to year.

SAP Developer Community Age Distribution

This shows increases in both the oldest age buckets for 2025. This was accompanied by similar decreases in the middle two age buckets ( ranging from 25 - 44 years of age). This merits deeper research as it potentially indicates either a gap in hiring younger employees to cover the normal aging of our community, or an outright loss of qualified younger staff. The two year trend in the 25-34 group reinforces that idea.

Where are the Developers?​

We asked, "Which country are you based in?"

By country, India has the largest number of respondents and probably the densest geographic concentration, followed by Germany, and then the USA. When grouped by SAP region, however, EMEA North is largest.

The proportions remain essentially unchanged from 2024.

Part II. Narrowing the Response Set

A primary objective of our survey is to build a model of our community's usage patterns. It helps us build more effective programs. With that goal in mind, in the remainder of the report we will narrow the data we'll consider in these ways:

  • External Developers Only - we exclude responses from SAP employees, and others (students, analysts, etc.)
  • Recent Development Experience - we only include responses from those who stated that they had been part of an SAP extension or integration project within the past year

When we filter the data set in this way, we are left with a set of 628 responses that we'll work with for the remainder of the report.

Workplace and Trends

Office Location
"As of right now, where do you primarily physically work from?"  
Year-to-year trend in Workplace
This shows a slowing trend of employees moving back to conventional offices from home. From a past Developer Survey, 29% of respondents reported they had primarily worked at home prior to the pandemic.

Recent SAP Project Experience?​

"Have you developed integrations or extensions for any SAP products or technologies in the past 12 months?" 

Extension and Integration are key scenarios for developers working with SAP applications. We wanted to focus on developers with recent experience in these areas. Roughly 70% of developers answered "Yes" here.

Popular Programming Languages​

"Which programming languages have you used in your development work over the past 12 months? Please select all that apply." 

This was posed as a multiple response question. ABAP dominates, as it has in every year of our survey. JavaScript is very popular as well, which could be attributed to its use in both SAPUI5 and CAP. Java and Python are roughly tied for third place. The relative positions of these top four languages hasn't changed much, year to year.

We extended our survey beyond just language use to ask about popular frameworks.

Popular Programming Frameworks​

We asked respondents who are Developers to pick which common front and back-end frameworks they have worked with in the past year.

Front-Ends

Back-Ends

Application and Business Technology Platform Architectures​

SAP applications can be extended using any one of several architectural approaches. These can be grouped into two major classes: on-stack (in-app) and side-by-side (essentially, BTP-based).

We were wondering which approaches are most popular.

Popular Extension Architectures

"For the most recent extension project that you were part of, what was the principal runtime architecture of the back-end elements?"  

We don't distinguish between newer and older SAP applications (e.g., S/4HANA vs. ECC) -- this likely explains the dominance of "on-stack ABAP". Aside from the obvious dominance of ABAP, we can also see that CAP is relatively popular. As with last year's results on this question, we see Low Code products like Build Apps and Build Process Automation are popular -- this question is asking about real production projects, and seeing those two account for roughly 10% of all extension projects highlights the value of these tools.

ABAP and CAP both have style or language variants in their respective frameworks. ABAP can be programmed as "ABAP for Cloud Development" or "ABAP Standard". CAP is available in JavaScript or Java language variants. Where the respondent selected ABAP or CAP, we asked about the variant used on the project:

 

ABAP Variants

CAP Variants

The rough 50-50 breakdown of ABAP styles is consistent with responses elsewhere in the survey. It reflects that roughly half of the ABAP projects are ECC or very early forms of S/4HANA. Importantly, the group that didn't understand the difference is considerably smaller than last year (2% versus 5% in 2024).

CAP/JavaScript is plainly most popular for CAP projects. Unlike last year, we do have respondents reporting the use of CAP/Java projects, although the percentage is modest - 5%.

Popular IDEs​

We are always curious about general work patterns of developers. We asked about developer's most commonly used editors. While it would be difficult in such a survey of all developers to add many questions, we were particularly curious about most popular IDE or editor.

 
Preferred IDE or Editor
"Which IDE or text editor do you most commonly use for development tasks?"  
Consistent with past year's results, the editor of choice will depend on the language. SAP GUI and -- arguably -- Eclipse are the primary editors for ABAP, so it's maybe no surprise that they are reflected as most popular. This is the first year that SAP Build Code appeared in the survey. We maintained separate choices for BAS and Build Code to see where folks might say that they have been using BAS, but have not yet transitioned to the broader capabilities offered by SAP Build Code.

Potential / Use of Generative AI for Developers​

Beginning with the 2024 survey, we asked developers about their thoughts and use of several major capabilities of Generative AI as it applies to software development.

Generative AI for Developers

"Which of these GenAI capabilities has your team explored or used? Select the choice that best describes your use of each capability. "  

An brief explanation of each category was included in the survey and is shown below:

Code Generation and Autocompletion: GenAI tools can generate code snippets, complete lines of code, or suggest entire functions based on the context provided by the developer.

Bug Detection and Code Review: AI models can be trained to identify potential bugs and vulnerabilities in the code. They can also suggest improvements in code quality, best practices, and code consistency.

Automated Testing and Test Data Generation: GenAI can be used to create test cases, which can help in thoroughly testing software applications. This includes generating test data that covers edge cases, potentially reducing the time and effort in manual testing.

Documentation Generation: AI can assist in generating and updating technical documentation. It can interpret the code and create comprehensive documentation, which is crucial for maintaining and scaling software projects.

Natural Language Processing (NLP) for Code: AI models can translate natural language queries into code, allowing developers to express their intent in plain language. This can be particularly helpful for novice developers working with unfamiliar frameworks.

Customized User Experience Design: AI can help generate user interface designs to enhance user experience.

AI-Powered Development Tools: Integration of AI in IDEs for real-time assistance, such as suggesting optimizations, refactoring code, or even predicting the next steps in development.

Automated Code Refactoring: AI can assist in code refactoring by suggesting or even implementing improvements in code structure.

Software Design and Architecture: AI can help in generating software design patterns or architectural models based on specified requirements. This can speed up the initial stages of development and ensure adherence to best practices.

Enhanced Security Protocols: Generative AI can be used to develop advanced security protocols and encryption methods, providing robust security solutions in software applications.

We also believed that Generative AI has been on the scene long enough for folks to being to experiment with putting it to use in meaningful ways. So, we included a couple of questions to gauge their perceptions of its utility to to their teams.

Current Gen AI Use

"How would you characterize your team’s current use of Generative AI tools or LLMs in your day-to-day work? Consider tools from any vendor or open source. Examples might include Joule, SAP Generative AI Hub, GitHub Copilot, or open source LLMs. (select the most applicable statement)"  

Gen AI Effectiveness for Developers

"Looking back on the past year, what is your own sense of how the use of Generative AI based tools or LLMs impacted the effectiveness or efficiency of your team?" 

Joule in SAP Build Code

Several Joule features were introduced into SAP Build Code prior to the survey. We asked respondents about their awareness or use of these features.

Joule for CDS Modeling

Joule for Test Data Set Generation

Joule Code Generation

Development Job Roles

We asked respondents which common development-related roles they perform.

"Which roles describe your day-to-day work? (Select all that apply)"

Respondents often "wear multiple hats" in their job. This question is asked in "select all that apply" form to capture that information.
The Venn diagram on the right depicts the relative overlaps of the top seven roles reported by respondents. The area of each overlapping region reflects the count respondents performing the overlapping roles. For example, from this we can seel that all (UX) "Designers" are also "Developers". Most but not quite all "Enterprise Architects" also are "Solution Architects".

These patterns of overlap are very similar to those in past years.

It is unsurprising that the "Developer" role leads. Architecture roles as a group have increased each year.

Development Domains​

For respondents selecting "Developer" as one of their roles, we were interested in the specific application Domains they work in:

"Which of the following development domains describe your day-to-day work? (select all that apply)"  
Year to year, Integration came in higher than we expected. Mobile web front-end development is more common than native mobile application development. This isn't completely unexpected, but worth noting.

 

Developer Satisfaction with SAP​

SAP Development Satisfaction
"For your recent experience developing an integration or extension, how would you describe your overall satisfaction with SAP?"  

Roughly 74% of respondents report they are “very satisfied” or “satisfied” with their development work with SAP. This is back near the 75% reported two years ago and also up from the 2024 figure, 70%.

We also gave all respondents a list of potential obstacles in their use of SAP tools and technologies and asked them to select the most significant pain points.

 
Obstacles in SAP tool us
"Which of these potential pain points are obstacles to your development when using SAP tools or frameworks? (select up to three most significant items)"  

BTP Guidance Framework

Awareness and Use of the BTP Guidance Framework
"The SAP BTP Guidance Framework was announced at SAP’s TechEd this past November. Which statement best described your level of familiarity with this document?"  
Nearly half of respondents were unaware of this document's release. Raising awareness for this and other related BTP framework documents would be a good goal for 2025.

Cloud Insights​

We first asked respondents if they used any cloud providers for their projects. For those that answered, "yes", we asked about the use of several major providers, including SAP BTP. We also asked if their use included SAP or non-SAP projects. This information was condensed into a chart depicting the relative use of each cloud provider.

Similar to the earlier questions around BTP Environments, these numbers do not reflect a tally of projects for each platform - instead it reflects developer exposure to each.

Using Cloud Providers?

"Are you using any cloud providers for your development projects?"  

We asked, "Which cloud providers are you using for development projects?" and we asked in a way to separate SAP-related projects from more general use.
 
SAP Business Technology Platform
Microsoft Azure

Amazon Web Services

 

Google Cloud Platform

Alibaba Cloud

Low / No Code Tools​

This is the third consecutive year in which we've asked questions around Low Code and No Code (LCNC) products. The list was composed of leaders in the then current Gartner LCNC Magic Quadrant Report and select SAP products. SAP Process Automation -- now Build Process Automation -- was not yet announced at the time of the 2022 survey, so it only appears in the more recent data.

For each product, we asked respondents to select the most applicable category reflecting their awareness or use. These questions were organized as a single large matrix of boxes in Qualtrics - There is no "I am unfamiliar with this product" response. Instead, the respondent simply would skip checking one of the other boxes.

It is important to point out that we are depicting response counts in the following charts rather than percentages and that the difference in total annual response counts must be considered to compare between years. The raw respondent count for 2023 was 2.4 times larger than 2022. And 2024 responses were less than half of 2023. We'll normalize for that in a later table.

These depictions can be misleading: our number of respondents each year varies. But we can normalize for that annual variation; what does this look like then? If we extract the data for production use for all products, we can use that to calculate the proportion use of each product by year (in other words, the sum of each column below totals one hundred percent). We can then visualize the relative shift in popularity over time:

 

Productive use of Product2022202320242025
Vendor A20%48%15%27%
SAP Build family (Build Apps + BPA)2%32%23%35%
Vendor B14%28%7%18%
SAP Signavio5%14%5%9%
Vendor C7%12%3%3%
Vendor D2%2%1%3%
Vendor E3%4%1%2%
Vendor F3%4%1%2%

SAP Build low code products dominate in our SAP ecosystem.

Learning and Help Resources​

We were interested in preferences in the format or media type of learning resources by respondents.

Preferred Learning Resources
"When learning new skills or technical subjects over the past 12 months, what resources did you tend use the most? Please select up to three resources."  

These results seem relatively unchanged year-to-year. One point of interest is the large difference in popularity between tutorials and MOOCs (53% versus 16% here). Tutorials tend to be hands-on. They are also broken into substantially smaller time chunks. This reinforces our hypothesis that different content types and required time investment have an impact in some learning scenarios. This merits investigation.

On the topic of Help, we shifted in this next question from formats to specific web sites.

 
Top SAP Help Resource Sites
"What is your preferred resource if you need help, or have a challenge with the SAP technology / tool you are working with? "  
Almost sixty percent of respondents say they use organic search or SAP Community as their primary source of SAP help. This is consistent with past years. This continues to demonstrate good visibility of the Community site overall and likely speaks well for the usefulness of the site content.

Satisfaction with SAP Learning Hub

SAP Learning Hub Awareness

"Which of the following best describes your exposure to the SAP Learning site?"

Overall Value of Learning Hub
"Overall how valuable are the resources on that site in helping you to understand SAP developer technologies?"  
Technical Certifications
"How many technology-related certification programs have you completed within the past four years?" 

Survey Methodology

This report is based on a Qualtrics web-based survey of 1,492 respondents from 58 countries. The survey ran for six calendar weeks between January 22 to March 7th, 2025. Ninety percent of the respondents invested ten minutes or less with the survey.

The survey was promoted via the SAP Community website and the SAP Developer Center, sap.com pop-up intercepts, social media posts.

Other References​

The annual Stack Overflow Developer Survey is an excellent (and no-cost) reference for industry-wide habits of developers.

entry.title: Unlock S/4HANA Cloud Secrets: Hybrid Events+APIs Strategy for Seamless BTP Data Flows
entry.content:
Strategic Financial Data Extraction from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to SAP BTP

Using Transfer Pricing as a Real-World Example

Transfer pricing represents a perfect use case to demonstrate generic financial data extraction strategies from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Multinational organisations need GL, Controlling, Material Ledger, and Asset Accounting postings to flow reliably to external engines hosted on SAP BTP—but Public Cloud’s “no direct database access” constraint demands architecturally sound patterns.

Why Transfer Pricing Perfectly Illustrates the Challenge

The business need: Calculate inter-company markups (cost-plus, resale-minus) using real-time ACDOCA postings across multiple currencies, profit centres, and cost elements.
The constraint: Pure cloud extensibility—no RFCs, no custom ABAP, no direct table extracts. 
The solution: Three proven patterns that work for any financial data integration scenario.

Pattern 1: Event Notifications (Real-Time Push Model)

How transfer pricing uses it: GL postings trigger automatic business event notifications routed through SAP Event Mesh to BTP applications.

 

Event Notification

Generic applicability: Works for any posting-driven process—revenue recognition, inter-company reconciliation, compliance monitoring. 

High-level activation:
  1. Integration configuration apps → Activate accounting-related event notifications
  2. Communication scenarios → Configure outbound event destinations
  3. Event Mesh subscription → Point to BTP service endpoint
  4. Payload contains: Company codes, ledger amounts (local/group), profit centres, material valuations

Pattern 2: Standard Query Services (Scheduled Pull Model)

How transfer pricing uses it: BTP service queries S/4HANA nightly for new/changed financial postings.

 

Query ServiceKey services (release-dependent availability):

  • Financial line item services → GL/CO/ML/AA postings
